question_text:
(a) Explaining your working and quoting standard results where required, find the rank and nullity of the following matrices.
$$X=\begin{pmatrix}1&2&-3\\ 2&-1&2\\ 3&1&-1\end{pmatrix}.$$
$$Y=\begin{pmatrix}1&1&-1\\ 1&2&0&3\\ 2&3&-1&4\\ 3&4&-2&5\end{pmatrix}.$$ [5]
(b) For each of the following matrices, say whether it is similar to a diagonal matrix over the reals. Justify your answers.
$$A=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\ -1&3\end{pmatrix}$$
$$B=\begin{pmatrix}-13&-28\\ 6&13\end{pmatrix}$$
$$C=\begin{pmatrix}-7&-5\\ 10&7\end{pmatrix}$$ [12]
(c) Write the symmetric bilinear form
$$F\left(\begin{pmatrix}x_{1}\\ x_{2}\end{pmatrix},\begin{pmatrix}y_{1}\\ y_{2}\end{pmatrix}\right)=x_{1}y_{1}+2x_{1}y_{2}+2x_{2}y_{1}-x_{2}y_{2}$$
on the space $V$ of $2\times 1$ column vectors over $\mathbb{R}$ in matrix form with respect to the usual basis $e_1, e_{2}$. Find a vector $e_{2}^{\prime}$ such that $\{e_{1},e_{2}^{\prime}\}$ is linearly independent and $F(e_{1},e_{2}^{\prime})=0$. For your $e_{2}^{\prime}$ find $F(e_{2}^{\prime},e_{2}^{\prime})$ and hence write down the matrix representing $F$ with respect to $e_{2}^{\prime}$. Is $F$ an inner product? [8]

In the following, $V=\mathbb{R}_{col}^{5}$ is the set of $5\times 1$ (column) matrices over $\mathbb{R}$, and the matrix $A$ is a $5\times 5$ matrix over $\mathbb{R}$ considered as a map $V\rightarrow V$ defined by $v\mapsto Av$.
(a) Say what it means for $v\in V$ to be an eigenvector of $A$ with corresponding eigenvalue $\lambda$. [2]
(b) If $v_{1},v_{2},v_{3},v_{4},v_{5}$ is a basis of $V$ where each $v_{i}$ is an eigenvector with eigenvalue $\lambda_{i}$ for $i=1...5$, write down the matrix representation of $A$ with respect to this basis. [2]
(c) Show that the minimum polynomial $m(x)$ of the matrix
$$A=\begin{pmatrix}2&0&0&3&0\\ 3&2&0&3&3\\ 0&0&2&0&0\\ 0&0&0&-1&0\\ -3&0&-3&-1\end{pmatrix}$$
has degree 2 and say what $m(x)$ is. [8]
(d) Find a basis of eigenvectors for the matrix $A$ above. Say what the eigenvalues corresponding to each of your eigenvectors are. [10]
(e) Hence write down a matrix $P$ such that $P^{-1}AP=D$ where $D$ is diagonal, and say what your $D$ is. [3]

(a) (i) Give the definition of a linear map.
(ii) Verify that the map $\phi:P_{1}\rightarrow P_{1}$ given by $f\mapsto xf^{\prime}+f(1)$ is a linear map. Here $P_{1}$ stands for the space of all polynomials of degree up to 1 and $f^{\prime}$ is the derivative of the polynomial $f\in P_{1}$. [5]
(b) Verify that the matrix of $\phi$ with respect to the standard basis of $P_{1}$ is
$$T=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\ 0&1\end{pmatrix}.$$ [4]
(c) (i) Which linear maps are called linear isomorphisms?
(ii) Using $T$ above, check whether the map from (a)(ii) is a linear isomorphism. [3]
(d) (i) Define the kernel of an arbitrary linear map $\psi:U\rightarrow V$.
(ii) Prove that $\psi$ is injective if and only if $\ker(\psi)=0$.
(iii) Based on your answer in (c)(ii), what can you say about $\ker(\phi)$? Explain. [8]
(e) (i) Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the matrix $T$ above.
(ii) Is $T$ diagonalisable? Explain. [5]

(a) (i) Give the definition of a bilinear form.
(ii) Under which conditions is a bilinear form called an inner product? [5]
(b) A symmetric bilinear form on a 2-dimensional vector space $U$ has the Gram matrix
$$\Phi = \begin{pmatrix}a&b\\ c&1\end{pmatrix}$$
with respect to a basis $\{u,v\}$. Find the entries $a$, $b$, and $c$ if it is given that $\Phi(u,u)=1$ and $\Phi(u+v,v)=0$. [4]
(c) Let $V$ be an inner product space.
(i) Which sets of vectors in $V$ are called orthogonal?
(ii) Prove that every orthogonal set of non-zero vectors is linearly independent. [6]
(d) Consider the space $P_{2}$ of all polynomials of degree up to 2 as an inner product space with respect to the inner product
$$(f,g)=\int_{-1}^{1}f(x)g(x)dx.$$
(i) Verify that the polynomials 1 and $x$ are orthogonal.
(ii) Find the orthogonal projection of $3x^{2}$ to the plane $W=\langle 1,x\rangle$. Clearly state which polynomial is the projection. [5]
(e) Consider $\mathbb{R}^{4}$ as an inner product space with respect to the dot product
$$u\cdot v=u_{1}v_{1}+u_{2}v_{2}+u_{3}v_{3}+u_{4}v_{4},$$
where $u=(u_{1},u_{2},u_{3},u_{4})$ and $v=(v_{1},v_{2},v_{3},v_{4})$. Use the Gram-Schmidt process to construct an orthogonal basis in the subspace $E=\langle (1,-1,0,0),(1,1,1,-1),(2,0,2,0)\rangle$. [5]

(a) (i) Give the definition of a linear map.
(ii) Verify that $\phi:P_{1}\rightarrow P_{1}$ given by $f\mapsto\frac{d}{dx}[(x-1)f]$ is a linear map. Here $P_{1}$ stands for the space of all polynomials in the indeterminate $x$, of degree up to 1. [5]
(b) Verify that the matrix of this with respect to the standard basis of $P_{1}$ is
$$M=\begin{pmatrix}1&-1\\ 0&2\end{pmatrix}$$ [3]
(c) (i) State the definition of the kernel of a linear map.
(ii) Determine the kernel of the map $\phi$ from part (a).
(iii) Is $\phi$ injective? Explain. [7]
(d) Suppose that $\alpha:U\rightarrow V$ and $\beta:U\rightarrow V$ are linear maps, where $U$ and $V$ are vector spaces over the same field of scalars $\mathbb{F}$. Show that $\ker(\alpha)\subseteq \ker(\beta)$ if and only if, for $u,u^{\prime}\in U$, the equality $\alpha(u)=\alpha(u^{\prime})$ implies $\beta(u)=\beta(u^{\prime})$. [5]
(e) (i) Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the matrix $M$ above.
(ii) Either find a matrix $T$ such that $D=T^{-1}MT$ is diagonal or explain why $M$ is not diagonalisable. [5]

(a) What is an inner product space? Your answer should include the definition of an inner product, but you may assume that the general concept of a bilinear form is known. [4]
(b) Let $V$ be an inner product space. For a subspace $W\subseteq V$, define the perp of $W$ as
$$W^{\perp}=\{v\in V|(v,w)=0 \text{ for all } w\in W\}.$$
Using the subspace criterion, show that $W^{\perp}$ is a subspace of $V$. [5]
(c) Let again $V$ be an inner product space.
(i) Which bases in $V$ are called orthogonal? Which bases are called orthonormal?
(ii) Suppose that $B=\{v_{1},v_{2},...,v_{n}\}$ is an orthogonal basis of $V$. Deduce the following formula for the $B$-coordinates $c_{1},c_{2},...,c_{n}$ of a vector $v\in V$:
$$c_{i}=\frac{(v,v_{i})}{(v_{i},v_{i})},$$
for $i=1,2,...,n$.
(iii) How does the formula simplify when the basis $B$ is known to be orthonormal? [6]
(d) Consider the polynomial space $P_{2}$ as an inner product space with respect to the inner product
$$(f,g)=\int_{0}^{1}f(x)g(x)dx.$$
(i) Verify that the polynomials 1 and $2x-1$ are orthogonal.
(ii) Find the length of $2x-1$.
(iii) Find the orthogonal projection of $x^{2}$ to the line $L=\langle 1\rangle$. Clearly state which polynomial is the projection. [5]
(e) Consider $\mathbb{R}^{4}$ as an inner product space with respect to the dot product
$$u\cdot v=u_{1}v_{1}+u_{2}v_{2}+u_{3}v_{3}+u_{4}v_{4}$$
where $u=(u_{1},u_{2},u_{3},u_{4})$ and $v=(v_{1},v_{2},v_{3},v_{4})$. Use the Gram-Schmidt process to construct an orthogonal basis in the subspace $E=\langle (1,-1,0,0),(1,-1,-1,1),(2,0,0,2)\rangle$. [5]

Let $(V,\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle)$ denote an inner product space with $\dim V=n$. Let $||\cdot||$ denote the norm induced by the inner product $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle$.
(a) Show that for all $u,v\in V$ there holds
$$||u+v||\le||u||+||v||$$
For which non-zero vectors $u, v$ does equality hold? [4]
(b) Let $S=\{v_{1},v_{2},...,v_{k}\}$ denote a set of non-zero vectors in $V$, orthogonal with respect to the inner product $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle$.
(i) Show that $S$ is a linearly independent set if $k\le n$.
(ii) Show that $0\in S$ if $k=n+1$. [6]
In the remainder of this question, we let $\mathcal{P}_{m}(\mathbb{R})$ denote the space of polynomials of degree $m$ with real coefficients. We define the inner product $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle:\mathcal{P}_{4}(\mathbb{R})\times\mathcal{P}_{4}(\mathbb{R})$ via
$$\langle p,q\rangle:=\int_{-1}^{1}p^{\prime}(x)q^{\prime}(x)dx+p(0)q(0),$$
where $p^{\prime}(x),q^{\prime}(x)$ denote the first derivatives of $p(x),q(x)$, respectively, with respect to $x$.
(c) Let $V=\mathcal{P}_{3}(\mathbb{R})$. Find a basis $B=\{q_{1},q_{2},q_{3},q_{4}\}$ for $V$ which is orthogonal with respect to the inner product $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle$. [8]
(d) Find a polynomial $q_{5}$ orthogonal to the polynomials $q_{i}(i=1,2,3,4)$ from part (c). Make sure you explain your working or justify your answer. [3]
(e) Find the orthogonal projection of $p(x)=x^{4}$ onto $V$ with respect to $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle$. [4]

Let $f:V\rightarrow V$ be a linear map, where $V$ denotes a finite-dimensional vector space over some field $\mathbb{F}$.
(a) Let $a\in\mathbb{F}$ and define $E_{a}:=\ker(a\cdot id_{V}-f)$, where $id_{V}$ is the identity map on $V$.
(i) Show that if a non-zero vector $v$ is in $E_{a}$ for some $a$, then $(a,v)$ is an eigenpair for $f$.
(ii) Show that $E_{a}\le V$.
(iii) Can we have $E_{a}=V$? If so, give an example; if not, explain why. [8]
Let now $V=\mathcal{P}_{2}(\mathbb{R})$ and let $f$ be given by
$$f(p)=(x-1)p^{\prime}(x),\quad p\in V.$$
(b) Show that $f$ is a linear map. [2]
(c) Write down the matrix representation $A$ for $f$ with respect to the power basis. [3]
(d) Write down the characteristic polynomial of $A$. Hence, find the eigenpairs of $A$. [9]
(e) Use the eigenvectors of $A$ to derive the eigenvectors of $f$. [3]
